name: Abu Mahdhura
epithet: The Boy Who Mocked the Adhan
title: Abu Mahdhura (RA): The Boy Who Mocked the Adhan
meta_description: The story of Abu Mahdhura (RA), the young man who mocked the call to prayer and was given it to carry. How the Prophet Muhammad saw his potential and made him the muadhin of Makkah.
keywords: Abu Mahdhura, story of Abu Mahdhura, muadhin of Makkah, adhan, call to prayer, sahaba stories, the firsts
lede: He was one of a handful of boys standing at the edge of a Muslim camp, laughing at the call to prayer. He did not know the man calling it, and he did not want to. He only knew the rumours his parents had passed down.
This is the story of Abu Mahdhura (may Allah be pleased with him): a young man who mocked the adhan, and was handed it to carry for the rest of his life.

sections.h2: A name that was almost lost
paras: We are not even certain of his first name. Some say it was Samura, some say Aws, some say Salama. What survived was his title, Abu Mahdhura, and the one thing he became known for.

He came from the area around Makkah and Taif, a region that the message of Islam had only just begun to reach. His own corner of the story begins in the worst possible way: as one of the people who could not stand the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

He was young. His dislike was not really his own. It was handed to him by his elders, who knew the Prophet ﷺ only as the man from Makkah who had come to cause them trouble, and then come back again. The boy had never heard the message. He had only heard the complaints.

It is worth pausing on that. He was not a hardened opponent who had weighed the truth and refused it. He was a child who had absorbed the mood of the adults around him. His heart had simply never been given a fair chance, and that, it turns out, is a very different thing from a heart that is closed.

The Prophet ﷺ had lifted the siege of Taif and was making his way back toward Madinah. With him travelled a group of people who had escaped the city and come over to him, and among them was Abu Mahdhura.

It was a tense road. These were the same lands where ambushes were common, and a sneak attack could come at any moment. The Prophet ﷺ travelled fully armoured, a general as much as a messenger, alert to everything around him.

There is a quiet detail worth noticing here. The Prophet ﷺ never actually entered Taif. He had once been driven from it and stoned, and now he was leaving again without setting foot inside. He was not going back to the place that had hurt him. He was simply bringing people out of it, and moving on.

Even so, the prayer did not stop. When the time came, the Prophet ﷺ ordered the adhan to be called. And out at the edge of the camp stood a small group of youths who decided this was something to laugh at.
Abu Mahdhura later described it plainly. As the adhan was called, the boys began to mock it from a distance, calling out the words back in a mocking way, laughing among themselves.

Then a stern voice cut across the camp. The Prophet ﷺ called them over. This was not the moment of someone wandering in to ask about faith. This was the opposite: a group of children who had not thought about the consequences, and had now been caught.

They came forward, all of them terrified, and stood before him. He told them to do again what they had been doing, to repeat the call. They glanced at each other and began, quietly this time, with none of the mockery left in their voices.
The Prophet ﷺ listened, then asked which of them had been the loudest in making fun of the call. Abu Mahdhura remembered the answer with a kind of rueful honesty: they all pointed to me.

It is a small, human detail that anyone who was ever a child will recognise. They had mocked together, but the moment there were consequences, the other nine pointed at the one beside them. There is a lesson in that alone: when fear arrives, friends will often hand you over to save themselves. The Prophet ﷺ let the nine go, and kept Abu Mahdhura.

Picture the scene from the boy's side. He was the one singled out, alone before a man he had just insulted, a man who was also a commander on a dangerous road. Think of what a general in that position could have done: a punishment, a captivity, something worse. He had every reason to expect the worst, and no reason at all to expect what actually came.

sections.h2: A hand on his head, and his heart
paras: What came next was nothing he could have imagined. The Prophet ﷺ drew him closer and told him to call the adhan again, the way he had been calling it, out loud. He began low, then his voice rose and filled out, and it became clear the boy had a beautiful voice.
When he finished, the Prophet ﷺ handed him a small pouch of silver and smiled. A gift for you, he said. The boy who had braced for punishment now stood there stunned, silver in his hands.

Then the Prophet ﷺ placed his hand on the boy's forehead and made a quiet prayer for him. He moved his hand down to his chest, over his heart, and prayed for him again, and the boy watched him the whole time. He prayed that Allah would bless him, again and again.

Abu Mahdhura would say afterward that the Prophet ﷺ had been the most hated person in the world to him before that. By the time those hands lifted, he was the most beloved person in the world to him. The change did not come from an argument or a threat. It came from being treated with a gentleness he had done nothing to deserve.

He asked to be taught the call properly, and the Prophet ﷺ went through the whole adhan with him, line by line, until he had memorised it. The boy who had been mocking those words an hour earlier now wanted to get every one of them right.

sections.h2: Make me the muadhin of Makkah
paras: What he said next took some nerve. This boy, who minutes earlier had been mocking the call, now asked the Prophet ﷺ to let him be the muadhin of Makkah. He had not yet prayed a single prayer.
The Prophet ﷺ agreed. The city had only just been opened, and a boy who had arrived hating him would leave carrying its call to prayer.

Before he had even prayed a single prayer, the Prophet ﷺ trusted him with the call of a whole city. He sent him back to where a leader had already been appointed over Makkah, and told that leader the call to prayer was now to be Abu Mahdhura's. From mocking it to owning it, in the space of a single afternoon.

In Makkah his voice became something people looked forward to. They would ask him to repeat the call just to hear it. He used a singer's voice for the sake of Allah, and he never gave up the post. As long as the Prophet ﷺ lived, Abu Mahdhura would not let anyone else take his place, calling all five prayers himself, afraid that if he stepped aside even once the honour might pass to someone else.

There was one part of him he would never touch again. The place on his head where the Prophet ﷺ had laid his hand, Abu Mahdhura refused to ever cut or even part. He trimmed everything else and left that alone, because the Prophet ﷺ had touched it and prayed for him there.

He lived a long life and taught students who carried his narrations forward. All eight of the reports that come from him are about the adhan, and nothing else. He had been given one task, and he kept to it. The way he learned the call, repeating certain phrases the way the Prophet ﷺ had taught him, became one of the recognised ways of giving the adhan, traced back through him to that road outside Taif.

The honour stayed in his family. Just as the key of the Kaaba remained with its keepers, the call of Makkah remained with the children of Abu Mahdhura, his sons and grandsons after him, for generations. For a long time, if you came to Makkah, the adhan you heard was given in his way, by his descendants.

He guarded it so fiercely that, late in life, when a new muadhin was sent during the time of Muawiya, he is said to have pushed the man into the well of Zamzam rather than give up his place. He even put his stubbornness into verse, swearing by his Lord and by the Prophet ﷺ that this call was his and no one would come between him and it. It is half funny and half moving: a man so in love with a single gift that he would not let it go for anything.

lessons_heading: What Abu Mahdhura's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story turns on a single choice the Prophet ﷺ made: to look at a boy who showed no interest in being guided, and see what he could become.

lessons.bold: See the potential before the person does.
lessons.text: Abu Mahdhura showed no willingness to be guided. The Prophet ﷺ looked past the mockery, heard the voice, and saw a muadhin.
A pouch of silver, a hand on the head, a quiet prayer. The boy who expected punishment was won over completely.

lessons.bold: Do not break a young person.
lessons.text: He had every reason to crush a child who had insulted him. Instead he gave him a task worth owning, and the boy owned it for life.

lessons.bold: A gift, once entrusted, is worth guarding.
lessons.text: He was given the call to prayer and never let it go, holding it with a love that lasted the rest of his days.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who became the muadhin of Makkah. He is counted among the two most famous callers to prayer alongside Bilal (RA). His given name is uncertain, but his title, Abu Mahdhura, is well known.

faq.q: Why did Abu Mahdhura first dislike the Prophet?
faq.a: He was young and had grown up among people in the region of Makkah and Taif who opposed the Prophet ﷺ. He had never heard the message itself, only the hostility passed down from his elders, so at first he mocked the call to prayer.

faq.q: How did Abu Mahdhura become a muadhin?
faq.a: After catching the boys mocking the adhan, the Prophet ﷺ had Abu Mahdhura repeat the call, heard his beautiful voice, gave him a gift, and prayed for him. The boy's heart changed completely, and he asked to be the muadhin of Makkah. The Prophet ﷺ taught him the full adhan and sent him to do it.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Abu Mahdhura?
faq.a: That potential can be seen in someone who shows none of it yet, that gentleness changes hearts more surely than punishment, and that a trust given with love can be carried faithfully for a lifetime.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It would have been so easy to deal with that boy harshly. He had mocked something sacred, in front of everyone, on a dangerous road. No one would have blamed a tired commander for putting him in his place. Instead the Prophet ﷺ heard a gift in the very voice that had insulted him, and he handed that boy something to be proud of.

Most of us meet a difficult young person sooner or later, in our families, our work, our own homes. The instinct is to correct, to shame, to write them off. This story asks for the harder thing: to look for the good buried under the behaviour, and to trust someone with it before they have earned it. There is also a quieter invitation in it for us. The next time the adhan reaches you, remember that the call you are hearing was once carried by a boy who began by laughing at it, and was changed by a single act of kindness. A single act of mercy turned a mocker into the man who called Makkah to prayer for the rest of his life. Carry that patience into one difficult moment today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Mahdhura (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, including the uncertainty over his given name, we have followed what is most widely reported.

slides:
A group of boys stood at the edge of the camp, laughing at the call to prayer.

The loudest of them would one day call Makkah to prayer for the rest of his life.

He did not know the Prophet ﷺ. He only knew the rumours his elders had passed down.

So when the adhan was called, he mocked it.

A stern voice called the boys over.

They came terrified. The Prophet ﷺ asked which of them was loudest.

The other nine pointed straight at Abu Mahdhura.

He braced for punishment.

Instead the Prophet ﷺ asked him to call the adhan again. His voice rose, low to high, and it was beautiful.

A pouch of silver. A smile. "A gift for you."

Then a hand on his forehead, and a hand over his heart, and a quiet prayer for him.

"He was the most hated person in the world to me," he said.

By the time those hands lifted, "he was the most beloved person in the world to me."

Minutes after mocking the call, he asked: **let me be the muadhin of Makkah.**

The Prophet ﷺ taught him the whole adhan and sent him to do it.

**Do not break a young person.**

He never cut the hair the Prophet ﷺ had touched. He never gave up the call.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
He started out as one of the boys laughing at the call to prayer. Abu Mahdhura (may Allah be pleased with him) did not know the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, only the hostility he had inherited from his elders.

When the Prophet ﷺ caught the boys mocking the adhan, he did not punish the loudest one. He asked him to call it again, heard a beautiful voice, gave him a gift, and prayed for him with a hand on his head and his heart. The boy who had hated him most came to love him most, and asked to be the muadhin of Makkah. He carried that call for the rest of his life.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/abu-mahdhura

email.subject: The boy who mocked the call to prayer
email.alt_subject: "He was the most hated person to me, then the most beloved"
email.preview: He was caught laughing at the adhan. What the Prophet did next changed his whole life.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The story of Abu Mahdhura (may Allah be pleased with him) begins in the worst possible way. He was one of a group of boys standing at the edge of a Muslim camp, laughing at the call to prayer.

He was young, and his dislike was not really his own. He had grown up among people who opposed the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and he had never heard the message itself, only the complaints passed down to him. So when the adhan was called on the road back from Taif, he and the other boys mocked it.

A stern voice called them over. They came terrified, and the Prophet ﷺ asked which of them had been the loudest. The other nine pointed straight at Abu Mahdhura. The Prophet ﷺ let them go and kept him.

He braced for punishment. Instead the Prophet ﷺ asked him to call the adhan again, the way he had been doing it. His voice rose from low to high, and it was beautiful. When he finished, the Prophet ﷺ handed him a small pouch of silver, smiled, and said it was a gift. Then he placed a hand on the boy's forehead and prayed for him, and a hand over his heart and prayed for him again.

Abu Mahdhura would say that the Prophet ﷺ had been the most hated person in the world to him before that moment. By the time those hands lifted, he had become the most beloved.

Minutes after mocking the call, the boy asked to be the muadhin of Makkah. The Prophet ﷺ taught him the whole adhan and sent him to do it. He never gave it up. He even refused to ever cut the part of his hair the Prophet ﷺ had touched.

There is a lesson here that is easy to miss. It would have been so simple to break that boy. Instead the Prophet ﷺ looked past the mockery, heard the gift in his voice, and trusted him with something worth keeping for life.

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us see the good in the difficult people we meet.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was one of a handful of boys standing at the edge of a Muslim camp, laughing at the call to prayer. He did not know the man calling it, and he did not want to. He only knew the rumours his elders had passed down.

Abu Mahdhura (may Allah be pleased with him) came from the region around Makkah and Taif. As the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ travelled home from the siege of Taif, the time for prayer came, and the adhan was called. Out at the edge of the camp, the boys began to mock it.

A stern voice called them over. They came forward terrified, and the Prophet ﷺ asked which of them had been the loudest. They all pointed to the same boy. The Prophet ﷺ let the others go and kept Abu Mahdhura, who stood there expecting the worst.

Instead, the Prophet ﷺ drew him closer and asked him to call the adhan again. His voice began low, then rose and filled out, and it was beautiful. When he finished, the Prophet ﷺ gave him a pouch of silver, smiled, and called it a gift.

Then the Prophet ﷺ placed a hand on the boy's forehead and prayed for him, and moved his hand to his chest, over his heart, and prayed for him again. Abu Mahdhura watched the whole time. He would say that the Prophet ﷺ had been the most hated person in the world to him, and in those moments became the most beloved.

Minutes after mocking the call, the boy asked to be the muadhin of Makkah. The Prophet ﷺ taught him the full adhan and sent him to do it. His voice became something the people of Makkah looked forward to, and he never gave the post up.
He never again cut the part of his hair the Prophet ﷺ had touched. He guarded the call to prayer for the rest of his life, and after him his children and grandchildren carried it for generations.
storybook.pullquote: He was the most hated person in the world to me. Then he became the most beloved.
storybook.reflection: It would have been so easy to break that boy. He had mocked something sacred, in public, on a dangerous road. Instead the Prophet ﷺ heard the gift in the voice that had insulted him and trusted him with it. We all meet a difficult young person sooner or later. This story asks us to look for the good buried under the behaviour, and to hand it to them before they have earned it.
